segments.title: Ellwood as a visible coastal target
segments.summary: The Ellwood oil field becomes a useful case study in why an undefended, visible industrial target could matter more psychologically than materially.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames Ellwood as an oil-field target whose location near Santa Barbara, corporate visibility, and communications made it likely to be noticed after a limited attack. He links the timing to Roosevelt's February 23, 1942 radio speech and places the raid within a small Japanese submarine deployment off the American West Coast. The watch point is useful for seeing how a minor bombardment could be chosen for strategic attention rather than battlefield damage.

evidence.note: The transcript describes a Japanese submarine attacking the oil field and explains why Ellwood was a noticeable target.
The transcript connects the raid to Roosevelt's speech and the limited Japanese submarine patrols off the West Coast.

segments.title: I-17 and Kozo Nishino
segments.summary: The I-17 section corrects popular stories about the submarine commander and highlights the boat's long-range weapons fit.
segments.body: This time note identifies I-17 as a large I-15 class submarine with torpedo tubes, a deck gun, and a carried seaplane. Dr. Clarke pushes back on the revenge-story version of Kozo Nishino, arguing from career logic that a valuable long-range submarine would not be entrusted to someone without submarine experience. He instead presents Nishino as a career submarine officer and one of the Imperial Japanese Navy's strongest submarine commanders.

evidence.note: The transcript lists I-17's torpedo tubes, deck gun, displacement, length, and commander.
The transcript rejects the merchant-captain revenge story and describes Nishino as a career submarine officer.

segments.title: What the Ellwood bombardment actually hit
segments.summary: The bombardment account follows I-17's night firing, the missed fuel target, minor physical damage, and the raid's classification as a deliberate foreign bombardment.
segments.body: The segment walks through I-17 stopping off the oil field, opening fire on a fuel-tank target, and causing confusion among a skeleton night crew before anyone recognized a submarine attack. Dr. Clarke stresses the practical gunnery problem: a submarine at night was a poor gun platform, so the shells destroyed or damaged relatively small targets while missing the main fuel tanks. He then explains why Ellwood is remembered as a deliberate bombardment of the United States rather than simply as a coastal incident.

evidence.note: The transcript describes I-17 opening fire on the Richfield aviation fuel tank area and the workmen's initial confusion.
The transcript explains the submarine gunnery limitations and lists the actual damage.
The transcript classifies Ellwood as an aimed bombardment and introduces the invasion-scare theme.

segments.title: Ellwood and the Battle of Los Angeles
segments.summary: The aftermath segment shows how the Ellwood raid fed reports, fear, and the deadly confusion remembered as the Battle of Los Angeles.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts Ellwood's lack of direct fatalities with the deaths that followed during the Battle of Los Angeles the next night. He uses Reverend Arthur Basham's report of a supposed enemy submarine moving toward Los Angeles to show how local sightings and fear amplified the raid's effect. The point is not that Ellwood caused major physical damage, but that it created a mental environment where coastal defense alarms felt plausible.

evidence.note: The transcript states that no one died at Ellwood but people died during the Battle of Los Angeles.
The transcript describes Basham's submarine sighting report and says I-17 had actually sailed west.

segments.title: Battle of Baltimore as invasion-scare memory
segments.summary: The Battle of Baltimore comparison explains the older American memory behind later fears of bombardment and invasion.
segments.body: This long historical comparison moves from the burning of Washington to the defense of Baltimore, emphasizing that the Americans brought stronger forces, more competent leadership, and prepared defenses than they had at Washington. Dr. Clarke describes the British land advance, Fort McHenry's role in denying naval fire support, and the decision to withdraw rather than risk a frontal assault. The watch point helps viewers understand why Ellwood and Fort Stevens could resonate with an older invasion-scare tradition rather than standing alone as isolated shelling incidents.

evidence.note: The transcript compares Baltimore with Washington and lists the American defensive forces.
The transcript discusses British expeditionary plans, veteran forces, and the possible strategic consequences of a larger British commitment.
The transcript follows the North Point action, Fort McHenry, British fire-support limits, and the withdrawal toward New Orleans.

segments.title: Fort Stevens and submarine gunfire limits
segments.summary: The Fort Stevens watch point shows a later Japanese shelling that produced more alarm than military damage.
segments.body: The Fort Stevens section follows I-25 as it approached the Columbia River and fired at an old coastal fort armed with much heavier fixed artillery. Dr. Clarke explains why the fort commander refused to return fire: the submarine was outside effective range, the base was blacked out, and the submarine was an unstable night gunnery platform. The result was minor damage, including telephone cables, but the episode reinforced American invasion fears and led to defensive measures along the coast.

evidence.note: The transcript describes I-25 surfacing near the Columbia River and firing at Fort Stevens.
The transcript explains why the fort did not return fire and lists the limited damage.
The transcript links the bombardment to coastal barbed wire defenses and the wider invasion-scare discussion.

segments.title: The Aleutians as the real invasion
segments.summary: The Aleutians chapter connects coastal bombardment fears to the actual occupation of Attu and Kiska and the hard logistics of the North Pacific campaign.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke pivots from invasion scares to the Aleutian Islands campaign, where American territory really was occupied and Dutch Harbor was attacked. He covers Kinkaid, Dutch Harbor, Japanese carrier strikes, Attu, Kiska, and the infrastructure problem created by Alaska's limited wartime links to the rest of North America. The later discussion of Operation Cottage and the Battle of the Pips shows how fog, mines, poor information, and radar uncertainty turned the campaign into a costly logistical and command problem even after Japanese forces had withdrawn.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the Aleutian Islands campaign as the real occupation of American soil.
The transcript discusses Kinkaid, Hasegawa, and the command logic around preserving Japanese ships.
The transcript describes Dutch Harbor, Japanese carrier strikes, the Akutan Zero, and the occupation of Attu and Kiska.
The transcript emphasizes Aleutian logistics and the Alaska Highway context.
The transcript covers Attu casualties, Operation Cottage, Kiska, and the Battle of the Pips.
